item.title: Example Page 1
item.description:
In this tutorial, I'll share my top 10 tips for getting started with Academic:

Tip 1

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Duis posuere tellus ac convallis placerat. Proin tincidunt magna sed ex sollicitudin condimentum. Sed ac faucibus dolor, scelerisque sollicitudin nisi. Cras purus urna, suscipit quis sapien eu, pulvinar tempor diam. Quisque risus orci, mollis id ante sit amet, gravida egestas nisl. Sed ac tempus magna. Proin in dui enim. Donec condimentum, sem id dapibus fringilla, tellus enim condimentum arcu, nec volutpat est felis vel metus. Vestibulum sit amet erat at nulla eleifend gravida.

Nullam vel molestie justo. Curabitur vitae efficitur leo. In hac habitasse platea dictumst. Sed pulvinar mauris dui, eget varius purus congue ac. Nulla euismod, lorem vel elementum dapibus, nunc justo porta mi, sed tempus est est vel tellus. Nam et enim eleifend, laoreet sem sit amet, elementum sem. Morbi ut leo congue, maximus velit ut, finibus arcu. In et libero cursus, rutrum risus non, molestie leo. Nullam congue quam et volutpat malesuada. Sed risus tortor, pulvinar et dictum nec, sodales non mi. Phasellus lacinia commodo laoreet. Nam mollis, erat in feugiat consectetur, purus eros egestas tellus, in auctor urna odio at nibh. Mauris imperdiet nisi ac magna convallis, at rhoncus ligula cursus.

Cras aliquam rhoncus ipsum, in hendrerit nunc mattis vitae. Duis vitae efficitur metus, ac tempus leo. Cras nec fringilla lacus. Quisque sit amet risus at ipsum pharetra commodo. Sed aliquam mauris at consequat eleifend. Praesent porta, augue sed viverra bibendum, neque ante euismod ante, in vehicula justo lorem ac eros. Suspendisse augue libero, venenatis eget tincidunt ut, malesuada at lorem. Donec vitae bibendum arcu. Aenean maximus nulla non pretium iaculis. Quisque imperdiet, nulla in pulvinar aliquet, velit quam ultrices quam, sit amet fringilla leo sem vel nunc. Mauris in lacinia lacus.

Suspendisse a tincidunt lacus. Curabitur at urna sagittis, dictum ante sit amet, euismod magna. Sed rutrum massa id tortor commodo, vitae elementum turpis tempus. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Aenean purus turpis, venenatis a ullamcorper nec, tincidunt et massa. Integer posuere quam rutrum arcu vehicula imperdiet. Mauris ullamcorper quam vitae purus congue, quis euismod magna eleifend. Vestibulum semper vel augue eget tincidunt. Fusce eget justo sodales, dapibus odio eu, ultrices lorem. Duis condimentum lorem id eros commodo, in facilisis mauris scelerisque. Morbi sed auctor leo. Nullam volutpat a lacus quis pharetra. Nulla congue rutrum magna a ornare.

Aliquam in turpis accumsan, malesuada nibh ut, hendrerit justo. Cum sociis natoque penatibus et magnis dis parturient montes, nascetur ridiculus mus. Quisque sed erat nec justo posuere suscipit. Donec ut efficitur arcu, in malesuada neque. Nunc dignissim nisl massa, id vulputate nunc pretium nec. Quisque eget urna in risus suscipit ultricies. Pellentesque odio odio, tincidunt in eleifend sed, posuere a diam. Nam gravida nisl convallis semper elementum. Morbi vitae felis faucibus, vulputate orci placerat, aliquet nisi. Aliquam erat volutpat. Maecenas sagittis pulvinar purus, sed porta quam laoreet at.

Tip 2

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Duis posuere tellus ac convallis placerat. Proin tincidunt magna sed ex sollicitudin condimentum. Sed ac faucibus dolor, scelerisque sollicitudin nisi. Cras purus urna, suscipit quis sapien eu, pulvinar tempor diam. Quisque risus orci, mollis id ante sit amet, gravida egestas nisl. Sed ac tempus magna. Proin in dui enim. Donec condimentum, sem id dapibus fringilla, tellus enim condimentum arcu, nec volutpat est felis vel metus. Vestibulum sit amet erat at nulla eleifend gravida.

Nullam vel molestie justo. Curabitur vitae efficitur leo. In hac habitasse platea dictumst. Sed pulvinar mauris dui, eget varius purus congue ac. Nulla euismod, lorem vel elementum dapibus, nunc justo porta mi, sed tempus est est vel tellus. Nam et enim eleifend, laoreet sem sit amet, elementum sem. Morbi ut leo congue, maximus velit ut, finibus arcu. In et libero cursus, rutrum risus non, molestie leo. Nullam congue quam et volutpat malesuada. Sed risus tortor, pulvinar et dictum nec, sodales non mi. Phasellus lacinia commodo laoreet. Nam mollis, erat in feugiat consectetur, purus eros egestas tellus, in auctor urna odio at nibh. Mauris imperdiet nisi ac magna convallis, at rhoncus ligula cursus.

Cras aliquam rhoncus ipsum, in hendrerit nunc mattis vitae. Duis vitae efficitur metus, ac tempus leo. Cras nec fringilla lacus. Quisque sit amet risus at ipsum pharetra commodo. Sed aliquam mauris at consequat eleifend. Praesent porta, augue sed viverra bibendum, neque ante euismod ante, in vehicula justo lorem ac eros. Suspendisse augue libero, venenatis eget tincidunt ut, malesuada at lorem. Donec vitae bibendum arcu. Aenean maximus nulla non pretium iaculis. Quisque imperdiet, nulla in pulvinar aliquet, velit quam ultrices quam, sit amet fringilla leo sem vel nunc. Mauris in lacinia lacus.

Suspendisse a tincidunt lacus. Curabitur at urna sagittis, dictum ante sit amet, euismod magna. Sed rutrum massa id tortor commodo, vitae elementum turpis tempus. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Aenean purus turpis, venenatis a ullamcorper nec, tincidunt et massa. Integer posuere quam rutrum arcu vehicula imperdiet. Mauris ullamcorper quam vitae purus congue, quis euismod magna eleifend. Vestibulum semper vel augue eget tincidunt. Fusce eget justo sodales, dapibus odio eu, ultrices lorem. Duis condimentum lorem id eros commodo, in facilisis mauris scelerisque. Morbi sed auctor leo. Nullam volutpat a lacus quis pharetra. Nulla congue rutrum magna a ornare.

Aliquam in turpis accumsan, malesuada nibh ut, hendrerit justo. Cum sociis natoque penatibus et magnis dis parturient montes, nascetur ridiculus mus. Quisque sed erat nec justo posuere suscipit. Donec ut efficitur arcu, in malesuada neque. Nunc dignissim nisl massa, id vulputate nunc pretium nec. Quisque eget urna in risus suscipit ultricies. Pellentesque odio odio, tincidunt in eleifend sed, posuere a diam. Nam gravida nisl convallis semper elementum. Morbi vitae felis faucibus, vulputate orci placerat, aliquet nisi. Aliquam erat volutpat. Maecenas sagittis pulvinar purus, sed porta quam laoreet at.

item.title: Example Page 2
item.description:
Here are some more tips for getting started with Academic:

Tip 3

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Duis posuere tellus ac convallis placerat. Proin tincidunt magna sed ex sollicitudin condimentum. item.title: Example Talk
item.description:
Click on the Slides button above to view the built-in slides feature.

Slides can be added in a few ways:

  • Create slides using Academic's Slides feature and link using slides parameter in the front matter of the talk file
  • Upload an existing slide deck to static/ and link using url_slides parameter in the front matter of the talk file
  • Embed your slides (e.g. Google Slides) or presentation video on this page using shortcodes.

Further talk details can easily be added to this page using Markdown and $\rm \LaTeX$ math code.

item.title: MIT 6.null-00 Course Overview and the Shell
item.description:
In traditional coursework, CS students are taught Data Structures, Algorithms, Operating Systems, Machine Learning, etc. However, there is no specific course on the tools that must be used to put this knowledge into practice. For example, once you get into Linux, you will definitely use the Shell, and if you want to track changes to source code, you can't get around git. Most students know about these tools through their classmates or teachers or by reading some blogs online. The result of this is that knowledge of the tools is very shallow and scattered.

MIT 6.null The Missing Semester of Your CS Education designed to help computer science students fill in gaps in their education that may have been missed in traditional coursework and build a systematic understanding of CS pratical tools. The course is taught by experienced instructors and includes video lectures, readings, assignments, and programming projects. And the course materials are available online, and students can work through the material at their own speed.

Personally, most of the content of the course has already been used in my work so I've had some practice, but I'm still hoping to review and develop systematic knowledge through this open course.

Overall, MIT 6.null The Missing Semester of Your CS Education is an excellent resource for computer science students who are looking to fill in gaps in their education and develop practical skills that will help them in their careers. Whether you are a current student or a professional looking to expand your knowledge, this course is well worth exploring. With its emphasis on practical applications and flexible, self-paced learning, it offers a unique and valuable learning experience for anyone interested in computer science.

The following output is based on the Tencent could server

[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos ~]$ cat /etc/redhat-release 
CentOS Linux release 7.6.1810 (Core)

NOTES

Core of Shells: allow you to run programs, give them input, and inspect their output in a semi-structured way.

The shell parses the command by splitting it by whitespace, and then runs the program indicated by the first word, supplying each subsequent word as an argument that the program can access.

ls command: To see what lives in a given directory.

Some other handy programs to know about at this point are mv (to rename/move a file), cp (to copy a file), and mkdir (to make a new directory).

man command: It takes as an argument the name of a program, and shows you its manual page.

In the shell, programs have two primary “streams” associated with them: their input stream and their output stream.

< file and > file: Let you rewire the input and output streams of a program to a file respectively.

| operator: lets you “chain” programs such that the output of one is the input of another.

sudo command: It lets you “do” something “as su” (short for “super user”, or “root”).

EXERCISES

  1. For this course, you need to be using a Unix shell like Bash or ZSH. If you are on Linux or macOS, you don’t have to do anything special. If you are on Windows, you need to make sure you are not running cmd.exe or PowerShell; you can use Windows Subsystem for Linux or a Linux virtual machine to use Unix-style command-line tools. To make sure you’re running an appropriate shell, you can try the command echo $SHELL. If it says something like /bin/bash or /usr/bin/zsh, that means you’re running the right program.
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos ~]$ echo $SHELL
/bin/bash
  1. Create a new directory called missing under /tmp.
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos ~]$ mkdir /tmp/missing/
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos tmp]$ ls -l
total 8
drwxrwxr-x 2 yuzhencode yuzhencode 4096 Feb 17 04:59 missing
  1. Look up the touch program. The man program is your friend.
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ man chmod
  1. Use touch to create a new file called semester in missing.
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ touch semester
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ ls -l
total 0
-rw-rw-r-- 1 yuzhencode yuzhencode 0 Feb 17 05:05 semester
  1. Write the following into that file, one line at a time:
#!/bin/sh
curl --head --silent https://missing.csail.mit.edu

The first line might be tricky to get working. It’s helpful to know that # starts a comment in Bash, and ! has a special meaning even within double-quoted (") strings. Bash treats single-quoted strings (') differently: they will do the trick in this case. See the Bash quoting manual page for more information.

[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ echo '#!/bin/sh' > semester 
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ cat semester 
#!/bin/sh
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ echo 'curl --head --silent https://missing.csail.mit.edu' >> semester 
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ cat semester 
#!/bin/sh
curl --head --silent https://missing.csail.mit.edu
  1. Try to execute the file, i.e. type the path to the script (./semester) into your shell and press enter. Understand why it doesn’t work by consulting the output of ls (hint: look at the permission bits of the file).
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ ./semester
-bash: ./semester: Permission denied
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ ls -l
total 4
-rw-rw-r-- 1 yuzhencode yuzhencode 31 Feb 17 06:38 semester
  1. Run the command by explicitly starting the sh interpreter, and giving it the file semester as the first argument, i.e. sh semester. Why does this work, while ./semester didn’t?
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ sh semester 
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Connection: keep-alive
Content-Length: 7987
Server: GitHub.com
Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8
Last-Modified: Sun, 12 Feb 2023 13:48:47 GMT
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *
ETag: "63e8ee3f-1f33"
expires: Thu, 16 Feb 2023 15:14:55 GMT
Cache-Control: max-age=600
x-proxy-cache: MISS
X-GitHub-Request-Id: BD92:11EC:CC0C5:1144EC:63EE4617
Accept-Ranges: bytes
Date: Sun, 17 Feb 2023 22:44:25 GMT
Via: 1.1 varnish
Age: 112
X-Served-By: cache-nrt-rjtf7700060-NRT
X-Cache: HIT
X-Cache-Hits: 1
X-Timer: S1676846665.485254,VS0,VE1
Vary: Accept-Encoding
X-Fastly-Request-ID: 224acc2983ac63337758858b6d8a36f3ada14784
  1. Look up the chmod program (e.g. use man chmod).
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ man chmod
  1. Use chmod to make it possible to run the command ./semester rather than having to type sh semester. How does your shell know that the file is supposed to be interpreted using sh? See this page on the shebang line for more information.
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ chmod +x semester 
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ ls -l
total 4
-rwxrwxr-x 1 yuzhencode yuzhencode 31 Feb 20 06:38 semester
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ ./semester 
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Connection: keep-alive
Content-Length: 7987
Server: GitHub.com
Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8
Last-Modified: Sun, 12 Feb 2023 13:48:47 GMT
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *
ETag: "63e8ee3f-1f33"
expires: Thu, 16 Feb 2023 15:14:55 GMT
Cache-Control: max-age=600
x-proxy-cache: MISS
X-GitHub-Request-Id: BD92:11EC:CC0C5:1144EC:63EE4617
Accept-Ranges: bytes
Date: Sun, 17 Feb 2023 22:42:33 GMT
Via: 1.1 varnish
Age: 0
X-Served-By: cache-nrt-rjtf7700073-NRT
X-Cache: HIT
X-Cache-Hits: 1
X-Timer: S1676846554.748478,VS0,VE222
Vary: Accept-Encoding
X-Fastly-Request-ID: fa9c19ae05a08a1a94ddd4ef81131b157124d936
  1. Use | and > to write the “last modified” date output by semester into a file called last-modified.txt in your home directory.
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ ./semester | grep --ignore-case last-modified > ~/last-modified.txt
[yuzhencode@VM-16-4-centos missing]$ cat ~/last-modified.txt 
Last-Modified: Sun, 12 Feb 2023 13:48:47 GMT
  1. Write a command that reads out your laptop battery’s power level or your desktop machine’s CPU temperature from /sys. Note: if you’re a macOS user, your OS doesn’t have sysfs, so you can skip this exercise.
C:\WINDOWS\system32>wmic /namespace:\\root\wmi PATH MSAcpi_ThermalZoneTemperature get CurrentTemperature
CurrentTemperature
3212

item.title: 01 k-Sum problem solution
item.description:
Programming is like a magic tool for me, I like to use it to solve all kinds of problems. I can always find something fun and learn something new from it.

This blog talks about the general solution for k-Sum problem. From 2Sum to 2+1Sum, then 2+1+1Sum. Utimately, the generic solution came out. k-Sum Problem is like Input n integers (positive and negative) and Decide are there k numbers in the input that sum to target integer?

Speaking From 2Sum Problem which is the most basic problem.

1. 2Sum

Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target. You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice. You can return the answer in any order.

Example 1: Input: nums = [2,7,11,15], target = 9 Output: [0,1] Explanation: Because nums[0] + nums[1] == 9, we return [0, 1].

1） Brute Force Solution

It is obvious that two nested loops can exhaust all sums.

def twoSum(self, nums: List[int], target: int) -> List[int]:
    for i in range(0, len(nums)):
        for j in range(i + 1, len(nums)):
            if nums[j] == target - nums[i]:
                return [i, j]

Time complexity: $$O(n^2)$$ Space complexity: $$O(1)$$

2） Use the Hashtable or Hashmap Feature of the Programming Language
def twoSum(self, nums: List[int], target: int) -> List[int]:
    map = {}
    for i, n in enumerate(nums):
        m = target - n
        if m in map:
            return [map[m], i]
        else:
            map[n] = i

Time complexity: $$O(n)$$ Space complexity: $$O(n)$$

3） Two Pointers Technique
def twoSum(self, nums: List[int], target: int) -> List[int]:
    nums_sorted = sorted(nums)
    lo = 0
    hi = len(nums) - 1
    while lo < hi :
        sum = nums_sorted[lo] + nums_sorted[hi]
        if sum < target:
            lo = lo + 1
        elif sum > target:
            hi = hi - 1
        elif sum == target:
            i = nums.index(nums_sorted[lo])
            if nums.count(nums_sorted[hi]) > 1:
                j = nums.index(nums_sorted[hi], i+1)
            else:
                j = nums.index(nums_sorted[hi])
            return i,j

Time complexity: $$O(n)$$ Space complexity: $$O(n)$$ Because this problem needs to return index, extra space is needed to store the sorted array. If the goal is to return numbers, the original space can be used to store them, so that the space complexity is $$O(1)$$.

2. 3Sum

Given an integer array nums, return all the triplets [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]] such that i != j, i != k, and j != k, and nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] == 0. Notice that the solution set must not contain duplicate triplets.

Example 1: Input: nums = [-1,0,1,2,-1,-4] Output: [[-1,-1,2],[-1,0,1]] Explanation: nums[0] + nums[1] + nums[2] = (-1) + 0 + 1 = 0. nums[1] + nums[2] + nums[4] = 0 + 1 + (-1) = 0. nums[0] + nums[3] + nums[4] = (-1) + 2 + (-1) = 0. The distinct triplets are [-1,0,1] and [-1,-1,2]. Notice that the order of the output and the order of the triplets does not matter.

1） Brute Force Solution

Three nested loops would exhaust all sums, which is obviously too complicated, so we drop this approach.

2） Converted to the TwoSum Problem with First Fixed Position
def threeSum(self, nums: List[int]) -> List[List[int]]:
    nums = sorted(nums)
    res = []
    target = 0
    for i in range(0, len(nums)):
        if 0<i<len(nums)-1 and nums[i]==nums[i-1]:
            continue
        tuples=twoSum(nums[i+1:], target-nums[i])
        for tuple in tuples:
            tuple.append(nums[i])
            res.append(tuple)
    return res
def twoSum(nums, target):
    lo = 0
    hi = len(nums)-1
    res = []
    while( lo < hi ):
        left = nums[lo]
        right = nums[hi]
        sum = left + right
        if sum < target:
            while lo<hi and nums[lo]==left:
                lo = lo+1
        elif sum > target:
            while lo<hi and nums[hi]==right:
                hi = hi-1
        else:
            res.append([left, right])
            while lo<hi and nums[lo]==left:
                lo = lo+1
            while lo<hi and nums[hi]==right:
                hi = hi-1
return res

Time complexity: $$O(n^2)$$ Space complexity: $$O(1)$$

3. 4Sum

Another similar problem on Leetcode is the 4Sum problem, and we can learn the idea of 3Sum to derive a solution.

4. k-Sum

Problem: k-Sum
Input:n integers (positive or negative)
Decide: Are there k (distinct) numbers in the input which sum to Target integer?

The number of K can be any number greater than 2, as long as it does not exceed the size of the array.

Constraints：

  • Whether the array is ordered or not？
  • Whether the elements in the array are distinct？
  • Whether the sub array is uniquely determined？

We use the generic approach, assuming that the array is unordered, that the array elements are duplicated and that the resulting subarray is not uniquely determined and may be 0 or more.

A generalized solution to the kSum problem using the idea of recursion.
def nSum(nums, n, target):
    nums = sorted(nums)
    res = []
    if n < 2 or len(nums) < 2:
        print("")
    elif n==2:
        lo = 0
        hi = len(nums)-1
        res = []
        while( lo < hi ):
            left = nums[lo]
            right = nums[hi]
            sum = left + right
            if sum < target:
                while lo<hi and nums[lo]==left:
                    lo = lo+1
            elif sum > target:
                while lo<hi and nums[hi]==right:
                    hi = hi-1
            else:
                res.append([left, right])
                while lo<hi and nums[lo]==left:
                    lo = lo+1
                while lo<hi and nums[hi]==right:
                    hi = hi-1
        return res  
    else:      
        for i in range(0, len(nums)):
            if 0<i<len(nums)-1 and nums[i]==nums[i-1]:
                continue
            tuples=nSum(nums[i+1:], n-1, target-nums[i])
            if tuples != None:
                for tuple in tuples:
                    tuple.append(nums[i])
                    res.append(tuple)
        return res

Time complexity: $$O(n^{k-1})$$ Space complexity: $$O(1)$$

item.title: An example preprint / working paper
item.description:
Click the Slides button above to demo Academic's Markdown slides feature.

Supplementary notes can be added here, including code and math.

item.title: Slides
item.description:
Create slides in Markdown with Academic

Academic | Documentation

Features

  • Efficiently write slides in Markdown
  • 3-in-1: Create, Present, and Publish your slides
  • Supports speaker notes
  • Mobile friendly slides

Controls

  • Next: Right Arrow or Space
  • Previous: Left Arrow
  • Start: Home
  • Finish: End
  • Overview: Esc
  • Speaker notes: S
  • Fullscreen: F
  • Zoom: Alt + Click
  • PDF Export: E

Code Highlighting

Inline code: variable

Code block:

porridge = "blueberry"
if porridge == "blueberry":
    print("Eating...")

Math

In-line math: $x + y = z$

Block math:

$$ f\left( x \right) = ;\frac{{2\left( {x + 4} \right)\left( {x - 4} \right)}}{{\left( {x + 4} \right)\left( {x + 1} \right)}} $$

Fragments

Make content appear incrementally

{{% fragment %}} One {{% /fragment %}}
{{% fragment %}} **Two** {{% /fragment %}}
{{% fragment %}} Three {{% /fragment %}}

Press Space to play!

One Two Three

A fragment can accept two optional parameters:

  • class: use a custom style (requires definition in custom CSS)
  • weight: sets the order in which a fragment appears

Speaker Notes

Add speaker notes to your presentation

{{% speaker_note %}}
- Only the speaker can read these notes
- Press `S` key to view
{{% /speaker_note %}}

Press the S key to view the speaker notes!

  • Only the speaker can read these notes
  • Press S key to view

Themes

  • black: Black background, white text, blue links (default)
  • white: White background, black text, blue links
  • league: Gray background, white text, blue links
  • beige: Beige background, dark text, brown links
  • sky: Blue background, thin dark text, blue links
  • night: Black background, thick white text, orange links
  • serif: Cappuccino background, gray text, brown links
  • simple: White background, black text, blue links
  • solarized: Cream-colored background, dark green text, blue links

Custom Slide

Customize the slide style and background

{{< slide background-image="/img/boards.jpg" >}}
{{< slide background-color="#0000FF" >}}
{{< slide class="my-style" >}}

Custom CSS Example

Let's make headers navy colored.

Create assets/css/reveal_custom.css with:

.reveal section h1,
.reveal section h2,
.reveal section h3 {
  color: navy;
}

Questions?

Ask

Documentation

item.title: An example journal article
item.description:
Click the Cite button above to demo the feature to enable visitors to import publication metadata into their reference management software.
Click the Slides button above to demo Academic's Markdown slides feature.

Supplementary notes can be added here, including code and math.
